dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems in [CITY], patient is 54F with chest and back pain.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
We called because she was having chest pain and shortness of breath. She's been like this for about an hour now.
She has not been sleeping well and called because of difficulty breathing in the hotel lobby.
Got it. Do you mind if we ask her a few questions directly?
Ms. Thompson, I'm EMT Jordan. We are here to help-you can tell us what's ailing you directly.
Ms. Thompson, can you tell me where you are and what happened?
I'm at the Days Inn in [CITY]. I've had a lot of chest and back pain.
That's helpful. What's your full name?
Linda. Linda Thompson.
What's the date?
Let me see… Today is December 18, I believe.
Good job. And what city is this in?
[CITY].
Linda, can you follow my finger with your eyes for me, please?
(Complies with eye tracking.)
Please touch your nose with the tip of your finger.
(Complies.)
Linda, we can now check your airway-can we do that?
Yes.
Your airway is patent and clear.
Partner, let's take a pulse oximeter and listen to her breathing.
O2 is at 100% on room air. She is breathing at 16 per minute, normal depth.
Linda, are you having any tightness in your chest?
I had a dry cough. Otherwise, I just feel too heavy to breathe.
Linda, is your skin warm and dry, or pale and clammy?
It feels normal.
Blood pressure is 143 over 80. Strong and full radial pulse.
Pulse is 63.
Let me listen to your lungs. Can I place the device on your back and front?
Yes.
Linda, deep breath for me. (Listens.) Clear bilateral lung fields. Good news-your lungs are open.
Let's talk about this pain. How would you rank the pain from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst you've ever felt in your life?
It's a 10 right now. It's non-radiating and in my chest and back.
Okay. When did the pain start?
About an hour ago.
What makes it better or worse?
Nothing helps. I feel it all the time right here (points to sternum and mid-back).
Linda, have you experienced chest or back pain before?
Yes. I have asthma and other medical issues. This feels worse than regular pain.
Do you have any allergies to medications?
Not that I'm aware of.
What medications are you currently taking?
Metformin, Depakote, Albuterol, and sometimes Glypizide.
Any episodes like this in the past-like a stroke or heart attack?
No.
Last time you had something to eat or drink?
I had a snack around noon with some alcohol and, I think, a bit of cocaine this morning.
What events led up to this, in your words? Can you explain what happened shortly before you called 911?
I was in the lobby, resting-just felt this pain build up and suddenly couldn't breathe so easy.
You mentioned the cough-did you find any blood or discoloration in it?
No. Just a regular, dry cough.
No signs of injury to the nose or mouth. Just a mild redness due to the dry cough.
Temp is 98 in the ear.
Blood sugar is 127.
EKG is reading sinus rhythm. No signs of any acute cardiac issues.
Blood pressure now is 134/84-mildly decreased since we first checked it.
Pulse is now 87, up from 63 earlier. Still within acceptable range.
Oxygen is at 98%. We'll keep an eye on it to make sure it doesn't drop too much.
The latest reading is still showing normal sinus rhythm. No ST-segment elevation recorded.
Let me feel your chest and back for areas of tenderness and abnormal movement.
Can you lie back and stay still while I assess your chest?
No deformities or palpable pain. Let's proceed with the 12-lead ECG as per protocol.
We're getting a 12-lead ECG now. It's part of our chest pain protocol.
Linda, we'll place these pads on your chest and arms. It's not painful, just make sure you stay still.
12-lead EKG results show normal sinus rhythm with a short PR interval but no acute cardiac findings.
We are going to give you an aspirin for comfort. It may help reduce your chest discomfort-can we do that?
Yes, sure.
Blood sugar remains at 127 with no change after aspirin administration.
Oxygen is 99%. We'll continue to monitor closely.
We're starting an IV in your arm-can we do that?
Yes.
Based on vitals and findings, we're using the cardiac suspected chest pain and general pain control protocols.
This is a non-urgent transport, but we will take you to [COMPANY_NAME]. Any issues with that?
Yeah, that's fine.
I need to secure you now. Let's get you onto the stretcher. Partner, assist me please.
We need five-point securing before we transport. Partner, make sure the straps are fastened correctly.
Patient is secured on the stretcher. She is safe and ready for transport.
We expect to arrive at [COMPANY_NAME] in 20 minutes. I'll call ahead so they can meet you with the right team.
Your latest vitals are 132 over 76, 76 HR, 99% SpO2. Pain is at a 10 and not spreading, and your ECG is clean.
Ma'am, you are going to be admitted for observation and follow-up imaging. Any legal questions or documents you'd like to be aware of?
No issues at all. Thank you for your help.
We are informing your admitting RN about your IV line and other vitals. This will speed up your care.
We encountered a motor vehicle collision during transport. We assisted victims before continuing. Another unit is managing the scene now.
She will arrive with no signs of cardiac distress or breathing issues. Skin is warm, no edema, and she denies nausea or vomiting.
I need to get your consent for treatment and transport. That's required by law before we proceed.
Of course. Sign me up.
Let the hospital know she answers questions well, remains oriented, and is very alert all the time.
She has a history of asthma, non-insulin diabetes, and other neurological conditions managed with medications.
Final impression-no changes in her status, steady breathing and vitals remain stable. We'll continue to monitor SpO2 and pulse waveforms during transport.
Provide further details for the RN regarding her history of bipolar disorder and reported chills. She denies fever or diarrhea and remains calm throughout the assessment and field treatment.
We also have a documented history of substance use, including cocaine and alcohol, which might be contributing to her symptoms today.
The patient denies weakness, dizziness, or bowel issues, and remains calm throughout the assessment and field treatment.
The 12-lead EKG revealed a normal sinus rhythm without ST-segment changes or other dysrhythmias. No ectopy found in this tracing.
The patient provided a voluntary consent for all medical treatment rendered and agrees to being transported to [COMPANY_NAME].
We'll transfer all care details to the on-site nurses. You will be under the care of the hospital shortly.
She has no breathing difficulties and her chest pain remains localized and non-radiating during our transport.
Vital signs are stable: BP 132/76, pulse 76, spo2 99%. We continue to monitor closely.
The patient has a documented medical history of epilepsy, alcohol dependence, and cocaine use, all of which are relayed to the hospital care team.
The patient has been handed off to the hospital staff, and [VEHICLE_IDENTIFIER] is returning to service for the remainder of the shift.
